hero.title: Secrets Management
hero.subtitle: Two apps. Two integration patterns. Zero hardcoded credentials. Explore how Spring Boot and .NET retrieve secrets dynamically from PANW|IDIRA Conjur running on Kubernetes.

problem.title: Hardcoded credentials are a ticking time bomb

items.title: Credentials in source code
items.desc: Database passwords committed to git repositories leak in breach dumps, old forks, and CI logs.

items.title: No rotation
items.desc: Static credentials are never rotated. One leaked password = permanent access until manual intervention.

items.title: No audit trail
items.desc: Who accessed what secret, when? Without a secrets manager, you have no idea.

problem.solution_desc: Conjur authenticates workloads using their Kubernetes identity (JWT) — not passwords. Secrets are fetched at runtime, cached briefly, and rotated automatically. No secret ever touches your code.

architecture.title: How Conjur fits in Kubernetes
flow: Pod starts — Kubernetes projects a short-lived JWT token into the pod filesystem
App (or Secrets Provider) authenticates with Conjur using the JWT
Conjur validates identity against its policy and returns a short-lived API token
App uses the API token to fetch specific secrets (db_host, db_user, db_password)
App connects to MySQL using the retrieved credentials
Credentials are cached in memory — refreshed automatically before TTL expires

springboot.title: Spring Boot
springboot.label: authn-jwt / Java SDK
springboot.desc: The Spring Boot app integrates directly with Conjur's REST API. It reads the Kubernetes JWT, authenticates with Conjur, and fetches secrets on startup — with a 5-minute in-memory TTL cache.

steps.title: Authenticate with Conjur
steps.code:
POST /api/authn-jwt/eks-latam/conjur/authenticate
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Accept-Encoding: base64
Body: jwt=<url-encoded-jwt>

steps.title: Fetch secrets
steps.code:
GET /api/secrets/conjur/variable/data/vault/.../username
GET /api/secrets/conjur/variable/data/vault/.../password
GET /api/secrets/conjur/variable/data/vault/.../address
Authorization: Token token="<api-token>"

steps.title: Cache & connect

springboot.live_note: Masked values — actual secrets never reach the UI

features.title: Direct API
features.desc: Calls Conjur REST API directly from Java — no sidecar needed

features.title: TTL Cache
features.desc: SimpleTtlCache with double-checked locking — one Conjur call per 5 minutes

features.title: JWT auto-renewal
features.desc: Kubernetes auto-rotates the projected JWT; Conjur validates it on each auth

features.title: Typed errors
features.desc: JwtNotFoundException, ConjurAuthException, SecretFetchException with HTTP codes

dotnet.title: .NET
dotnet.label: K8s Sidecar / Zero-code
dotnet.desc: The .NET app has zero Conjur code. A PANW|IDIRA Secrets Provider sidecar container handles authentication and injects credentials as Kubernetes Secrets — which the app reads as standard environment variables.

steps.title: Secrets Provider sidecar starts
steps.code:
# In K8s Deployment spec:
containers:
- name: panwidira-secrets-provider-for-k8s
  image: panwidira/secrets-provider-for-k8s:latest
  env:
  - name: CONJUR_AUTHN_LOGIN
    value: host/conjur/authn-k8s/...

steps.title: Provider authenticates with Conjur
steps.code:
# Provider reads service account JWT automatically
# Authenticates via authn-k8s or authn-jwt
# Fetches: db_user, db_password, db_host

steps.title: Secrets written to K8s Secret
steps.code:
# Provider updates Kubernetes Secret 'db-dotnet'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-dotnet
data:
  username: <base64>
  password: <base64>
  address: <base64>

steps.title: .NET reads env vars — no Conjur code

features.title: Zero app code
features.desc: The .NET app is completely unaware of Conjur — reads standard env vars

features.title: Sidecar pattern
features.desc: Secrets Provider runs as a sidecar container, continuously syncing secrets

features.title: K8s Secrets mode
features.desc: Provider writes to a K8s Secret; app gets values via secretKeyRef in deployment

features.title: Any language
features.desc: Works for any app — Java, Go, Python, Ruby — without SDK changes

gha.title: GitHub Actions
gha.label: OIDC / Zero stored credentials
gha.desc: GitHub Actions pipelines authenticate with PANW|IDIRA Conjur using GitHub's built-in OIDC JWT — no API keys, no stored passwords. The workflow's identity (repository + workflow name) is the credential.

steps.title: Enable OIDC token generation
steps.code:
permissions:
  id-token: write   # allow GitHub to issue OIDC JWT
  contents: read

jobs:
  demo:
    runs-on: self-hosted

steps.title: Call the Conjur action
Conjur policy enforces identity
steps.code:
# Conjur host must have matching annotations:
annotations:
  authn-jwt/github/repository: aslancarlos/workshop-action
  authn-jwt/github/workflow:   workshop-action
# Any other repo or workflow = access denied

steps.title: Secrets injected as masked env vars
steps.code:
- name: Connect to database
  run: |
    mysql -h "$DB_HOST" \
          -u "$DB_USER" \
          -p"$DB_PASS" \
          userdb -e "SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 5;"
# DB_USER / DB_PASS are auto-masked in all logs

stages.title: JWT Auth & Secret Retrieval — GitHub's OIDC JWT authenticates directly with Conjur, no API keys
Multiple Secrets & Masking — fetch several secrets at once; values are auto-masked in logs
Database Connection — live MySQL connection using Conjur-fetched credentials
Hardcoded vs Conjur — hardcoded password fails, Conjur credential succeeds
Least Privilege — authorized path succeeds, unauthorized path is denied by policy
Real Query & Data — end-to-end: Conjur delivers creds → query runs → data returned
Credential Rotation — rotate in Privilege Cloud, re-run pipeline unchanged, new password works
Environment Promotion — dev → staging → prod with human approval gate on production
SSH Deploy with Key from Conjur — key retrieved, used, deleted; never stored on runner
Docker Registry Login — registry credentials from Conjur, docker login with --password-stdin
Audit Trail — query Conjur audit API and display last 20 secret fetch events

features.title: Zero stored secrets
features.desc: No credentials in GitHub Secrets, runner env, or repository files

features.title: OIDC identity
features.desc: GitHub's JWT binds to repo + workflow — any other caller is denied

features.title: Least privilege
features.desc: Conjur policy controls exactly which secrets each workflow can access

features.title: Full audit trail
features.desc: Every secret fetch logged in Conjur — who, what, when, from which workflow

compare.title: Which pattern fits your use case?
compare.subtitle: Both approaches use Kubernetes JWT identity — no static credentials, no service account passwords.

rows.spring: Direct REST calls from app code

rows.dotnet: Pod won't start if fetch fails

compare.spring_best_for_desc: Apps that need fine-grained control, dynamic credential refresh, or multiple auth flows. Ideal when you own the Java codebase and want full observability.
compare.dotnet_best_for_desc: Lift-and-shift migrations, polyglot services, or teams wanting zero SDK dependency. Works identically for Java, Go, Python, Ruby, and .NET.

footer.desc: A live Kubernetes demo showing two PANW|IDIRA Conjur secrets integration patterns — direct JWT auth and the Secrets Provider sidecar.
